mission.title: Install and Configure Wasmcloud on Kubernetes
mission.description: wasmCloud is an open source Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) project that enables teams to build, manage, and scale polyglot Wasm apps across any cloud, K8s, or edge. Installing wasmCloud allows you to leverage its capabilities for creating scalable and portable applications without vendor lock-in.

steps.title: Add the wasmCloud Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official wasmCloud Helm repository to your Helm client using the following command:
```bash
helm repo add wasmcloud https://wasmcloud.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install wasmCloud using Helm
steps.description:
Install the wasmCloud chart with the following command, specifying the desired namespace:
```bash
helm install wasmcloud wasmcloud/benchmark --namespace wasmcloud --create-namespace --version 0.2.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check the status of the pods to ensure that wasmCloud is running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace wasmcloud
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the Helm release to set resource limits for the wasmCloud deployment. You can do this by updating the values.yaml or using the command:
```bash
helm upgrade wasmcloud wasmcloud/benchmark --namespace wasmcloud --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
To access the wasmCloud service, set up port forwarding:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/wasmcloud 8080:80 --namespace wasmcloud
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of wasmCloud will show the pods running in the 'wasmcloud' namespace when you execute `kubectl get pods --namespace wasmcloud`. You will also be able to access the service via port 8080 on your local machine.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add wasmcloud https://wasmcloud.github.io/charts
helm repo update
helm upgrade wasmcloud wasmcloud/benchmark --namespace wasmcloud --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the wasmCloud release from the specified namespace using Helm. This will remove the deployment and associated resources managed by Helm.
```bash
helm uninstall wasmcloud --namespace wasmcloud
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation. This ensures that all resources are cleaned up.
```bash
kubectl delete crd wasmclouds.wasmcloud.net
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace wasmcloud
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the wasmcloud namespace and verify that it has been removed. This step ensures that all resources associated with the namespace are cleaned up.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace wasmcloud
kubectl get namespaces | grep wasmcloud
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current state of the release. This can be done by exporting the current configuration.
```bash
helm get values wasmcloud --namespace wasmcloud > wasmcloud-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, then upgrade the wasmCloud release to the new version.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade wasmcloud wasmcloud/benchmark --namespace wasmcloud --version 0.2.2
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the pods to ensure that the upgrade was successful and that all pods are running as expected.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace wasmcloud
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This issue occurs when a pod fails to start properly and keeps crashing. To diagnose, check the logs of the pod:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace wasmcloud
```
Look for error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the wasmCloud service is not reachable, verify that the port forwarding is set up correctly. Check the service status:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace wasmcloud
```
Ensure that the correct ports are being forwarded and that the service is running.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to resource limits, check the resource usage of the pods:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace wasmcloud
```
If necessary, adjust the resource limits in the Helm values and upgrade the release.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive an error about version conflicts during an upgrade, ensure that you are using a compatible version of the chart. Check the available versions:
```bash
helm search repo wasmcloud/benchmark --versions
```
If needed, specify a different version in the upgrade command.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
